Physics Problems: Statistics and HTML

You said

can you solve all 8 problems step by step and give clear explanation of each step?

Here are the step-by-step solutions to your problem set. The mathematical derivations and statistical interpretations are broken down for clarity, and the HTML applications for the final three problems are provided as functional code blocks.

Problem 1 – Descriptive statistics of a measurement series

  1. Arithmetic Mean: The sum of the 8 measurements is 96.80 cm.

    xˉ=n∑xi​​=896.80​=12.10

    The arithmetic mean is 12.10 cm.

  2. Sample Variance and Standard Deviation: The sample variance is calculated using s2=n−11​∑(xi​−xˉ)2. The squared deviations from the mean (12.10) for each data point are: 0, 0.0004, 0.0004, 0.0001, 0.0001, 0.0009, 0.0009, 0. The sum of these squared deviations is 0.0028.

    s2=70.0028​=0.0004
    s=0.0004​=0.02

    The sample standard deviation s is 0.02 cm.

  3. Uncertainty of the Mean:

    u(xˉ)=n​s​=8​0.02​≈0.0071

    The uncertainty of the mean is 0.007 cm (rounded to one significant figure for uncertainty).

  4. Result Format: Matching the precision of the uncertainty:

    x=12.100±0.007

  5. Difference between s and u(xˉ): The standard deviation s measures the spread of the individual measurements (how much a single new measurement is expected to deviate from the mean). The uncertainty of the mean u(xˉ) measures the precision of the calculated mean itself (how close our calculated mean is to the true population mean). As you take more measurements, s stabilizes, but u(xˉ) shrinks.

  6. Confidence Intervals (Assuming Normal Distribution):

    • xˉ±s→[12.08, 12.12] (Approx. 68% confidence level: 68% of individual measurements fall in this range).

    • xˉ±2s→[12.06, 12.14] (Approx. 95% confidence level: 95% of measurements fall in this range).

    • xˉ±3s→[12.04, 12.16] (Approx. 99.7% confidence level, often rounded to 99 in standard heuristics).

Problem 2 – Propagation of uncertainty (Total Differential Method)

  1. Calculate Resistance R:

    R=IU​=0.4825.23​≈10.85

    The resistance is 10.85 Ω.

  2. Relative Uncertainty Formula (Total Differential Method): The total differential for R=U/I is:

    dR=∂U∂R​dU+∂I∂R​dI=I1​dU−I2U​dI

    Transitioning to maximum absolute uncertainty (replacing differentials with absolute uncertainties and ensuring all terms add up to represent the worst-case error):

    ΔR=​I1​​ΔU+​−I2U​​ΔI

    Divide both sides by R (which is U/I) to get the relative uncertainty:

    RΔR​=UΔU​+IΔI​

  3. Calculate Relative Uncertainty:

    • Voltage contribution: 0.04/5.23≈0.0076 (0.76%)

    • Current contribution: 0.006/0.482≈0.0124 (1.24%)

    • Total relative uncertainty: 0.0076+0.0124=0.0200 (2.0%)

    The current (I) dominates the uncertainty because its relative error (1.24%) is larger than the voltage's relative error (0.76%).

Problem 3 – Propagation of uncertainty (Density)

  1. Calculate Density ρ:

    ρ=abcm​=5.20×2.10×1.50128.4​=16.38128.4​≈7.84

    The density is 7.84 g/cm³.

  2. Relative Uncertainty Formula (Total Differential Method): Using the exact same logarithmic derivation / total differential principle as Problem 2 for multiplicative/divisional formulas:

    ρΔρ​=mΔm​+aΔa​+bΔb​+cΔc​

  3. Largest Contribution to Uncertainty: Let's calculate the relative uncertainty for each variable:

    • Δm/m=0.2/128.4≈0.0016 (0.16%)

    • Δa/a=0.02/5.20≈0.0038 (0.38%)

    • Δb/b=0.02/2.10≈0.0095 (0.95%)

    • Δc/c=0.01/1.50≈0.0067 (0.67%)

    The measurement of side b gives the largest contribution to the total relative uncertainty.

Problem 4 – Linear regression and acceleration due to gravity

  1. Linear Transformation: Let y=h and x=t2. The model h=21​gt2 becomes y=kx, where k=21​g. Calculated x values (t2): 0.0408, 0.0824, 0.1232, 0.1632, 0.2043.

  2. Linear Regression: Performing standard least squares regression for y=kx+b:

    • Mean of x (xˉ) = 0.1228

    • Mean of y (yˉ​) = 0.6000

    • Slope k=∑(xi​−xˉ)2∑(xi​−xˉ)(yi​−yˉ​)​≈4.904

  3. Determine g:

    g=2k=2×4.904=9.808

    The acceleration due to gravity is approximately 9.81 m/s².

  4. Estimate Uncertainty u(g): Using the standard error of the slope (sk​):

    • Residual standard error sy​≈0.0023

    • u(k)=∑(xi​−xˉ)2​sy​​≈0.018

    • u(g)=2×u(k)=2×0.018=0.036

    g=9.81±0.04 m/s².

  5. Goodness of Fit (R2): Calculating R2=1−SSTSSR​ yields R2=0.9999. This indicates an exceptionally good fit; the model explains over 99.9% of the variance in height.

Problem 5 – Systematic vs. statistical errors

  1. Group Calculations:

    • Group A:

      • Mean = 10.00

      • Standard Deviation (s) = 0.0158

      • Uncertainty of Mean (u(xˉ)) = 0.007

    • Group B:

      • Mean = 10.41

      • Standard Deviation (s) = 0.0158

      • Uncertainty of Mean (u(xˉ)) = 0.007

    • Group C:

      • Mean = 10.10

      • Standard Deviation (s) = 0.387

      • Uncertainty of Mean (u(xˉ)) = 0.173

  2. Comparison with True Value (10.00):

    • Group A is highly accurate and precise (matches the true value).

    • Group B is highly precise but wildly inaccurate (misses the true value completely).

    • Group C is accurate (the true value falls well within its confidence interval) but lacks precision.

  3. Error Dominance:

    • Group A: Statistical error dominates (no visible systematic shift).

    • Group B: Systematic error dominates (there is a clear ~0.41 offset).

    • Group C: Statistical error dominates (high variance masks any minor systematic shifts).

  4. Causes of Systematic Error:

    • A poorly calibrated instrument (e.g., a ruler whose "zero" mark is worn away, leading to a constant offset).

    • Environmental factors (e.g., thermal expansion of the rod if measured in a very hot room).

  5. Why increasing measurements fails for systematic error: Taking more measurements only shrinks the statistical variance (u(xˉ)∝1/n​). It does absolutely nothing to correct a fundamental flaw in the measurement tool or methodology, meaning you will just get highly precise, consistently wrong answers.

  6. Reflection: The result x=10.00±0.20 is generally considered "better" in science. While it is less precise, it is an accurate and honest representation of reality—the true value falls within its stated bounds. The result x=10.41±0.01 is misleading and dangerous; it implies high confidence in a completely incorrect value due to an uncorrected systematic bias.

Problem 6 (HTML) – Central Limit Theorem

Save the following code as an .html file and open it in any web browser. It uses Chart.js to handle the histogram plotting.

HTML
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>CLT Simulator</title>
    <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/chart.js"></script>
    <style>body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 20px; max-width: 800px; margin: auto; }</style>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Central Limit Theorem Simulator</h2>
    <label>Distribution: 
        <select id="dist"><option value="uniform">Uniform</option><option value="exponential">Exponential</option></select>
    </label>
    <label>Sample Size (n): <input type="number" id="nSize" value="30"></label>
    <label>Samples (N): <input type="number" id="NSamples" value="1000"></label>
    <button onclick="runSim()">Simulate</button>
    <p id="stats"></p>
    <canvas id="myChart"></canvas>

    <script>
        let chart;
        function getRandom(dist) {
            if (dist === 'uniform') return Math.random(); // Mean 0.5, Var 1/12
            if (dist === 'exponential') return -Math.log(1 - Math.random()); // Mean 1, Var 1
        }
        function runSim() {
            const dist = document.getElementById('dist').value;
            const n = parseInt(document.getElementById('nSize').value);
            const N = parseInt(document.getElementById('NSamples').value);
            let means = [];
            
            for(let i=0; i<N; i++) {
                let sum = 0;
                for(let j=0; j<n; j++) sum += getRandom(dist);
                means.push(sum / n);
            }
            
            const empiricalMean = means.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0)/N;
            const empiricalStd = Math.sqrt(means.reduce((a,b)=>a+Math.pow(b-empiricalMean,2),0)/(N-1));
            const theoreticalStd = dist === 'uniform' ? Math.sqrt((1/12)/n) : Math.sqrt(1/n);

            document.getElementById('stats').innerText = `Empirical Std: ${empiricalStd.toFixed(4)} | Theoretical Std: ${theoreticalStd.toFixed(4)}`;

            // Simple binning for histogram
            const bins = 30;
            const min = Math.min(...means), max = Math.max(...means);
            const binWidth = (max - min) / bins;
            let counts = new Array(bins).fill(0);
            means.forEach(val => {
                let idx = Math.floor((val - min) / binWidth);
                if(idx >= bins) idx = bins - 1;
                counts[idx]++;
            });

            const labels = counts.map((_, i) => (min + i * binWidth).toFixed(2));
            
            if(chart) chart.destroy();
            const ctx = document.getElementById('myChart').getContext('2d');
            chart = new Chart(ctx, {
                type: 'bar',
                data: { labels: labels, datasets: [{ label: 'Frequency', data: counts, backgroundColor: 'blue' }] }
            });
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>

Conclusions to add in your submission: As sample size n increases, the histogram of sample means becomes increasingly bell-shaped (Gaussian), regardless of the underlying source distribution. The spread of this distribution shrinks strictly according to σ/n​.

Problem 7 (HTML) – Simple pendulum: measurement of g

Save the following code as pendulum.html. It uses spacebar listener events to calculate periods and update the statistical derivations in real-time.

HTML
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>Pendulum g Measurement</title>
    <style>body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 20px; }</style>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Pendulum: Measure g</h2>
    <label>Length L (m): <input type="number" id="len" step="0.01" value="1.00"></label>
    <label>u(L) (m): <input type="number" id="uLen" step="0.001" value="0.01"></label>
    <p><i>Press Spacebar to start and log passes through the minimum.</i></p>
    <ul id="times"></ul>
    <div id="results"></div>

    <script>
        let times = [];
        let periods = [];
        document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e) {
            if (e.code === 'Space') {
                e.preventDefault();
                times.push(performance.now() / 1000); // in seconds
                updateApp();
            }
        });

        function updateApp() {
            if (times.length < 2) return;
            periods = [];
            for(let i=1; i<times.length; i++) {
                periods.push(times[i] - times[i-1]);
            }
            document.getElementById('times').innerHTML = periods.map((p,i) => `<li>T${i+1}: ${p.toFixed(3)} s</li>`).join('');
            
            if (periods.length >= 5) calculateG();
        }

        function calculateG() {
            const L = parseFloat(document.getElementById('len').value);
            const uL = parseFloat(document.getElementById('uLen').value);
            const n = periods.length;
            
            const T_mean = periods.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0) / n;
            const s_T = Math.sqrt(periods.reduce((a,b)=>a+Math.pow(b-T_mean,2),0) / (n-1));
            const uT = s_T / Math.sqrt(n);
            
            const g = 4 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) * L / Math.pow(T_mean, 2);
            
            const dg_dL = 4 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) / Math.pow(T_mean, 2);
            const dg_dT = -8 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) * L / Math.pow(T_mean, 3);
            
            const ug = Math.sqrt(Math.pow(dg_dL * uL, 2) + Math.pow(dg_dT * uT, 2));
            
            const pL = (Math.pow(dg_dL * uL, 2) / Math.pow(ug, 2) * 100).toFixed(1);
            const pT = (Math.pow(dg_dT * uT, 2) / Math.pow(ug, 2) * 100).toFixed(1);

            document.getElementById('results').innerHTML = `
                <h3>Results (n=${n}):</h3>
                <p>Mean Period (T): ${T_mean.toFixed(3)} ± ${uT.toFixed(3)} s</p>
                <p><strong>g = ${g.toFixed(2)} ± ${ug.toFixed(2)} m/s²</strong></p>
                <p>Uncertainty Contribution: L (${pL}%), T (${pT}%)</p>
            `;
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>

Problem 8 (HTML) – Mass-spring oscillator: frequency and k

Save the following code as oscillator.html. It mirrors the logic of Problem 7 but applies the derivations for spring constant k and frequency f.

HTML
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <title>Oscillator k Measurement</title>
    <style>body { font-family: sans-serif; padding: 20px; }</style>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Mass-Spring: Measure k</h2>
    <label>Mass m (kg): <input type="number" id="mass" step="0.01" value="0.50"></label>
    <label>u(m) (kg): <input type="number" id="uMass" step="0.001" value="0.005"></label>
    <p><i>Press Spacebar to start and log passes through the minimum.</i></p>
    <ul id="times"></ul>
    <div id="results"></div>

    <script>
        let times = [];
        let periods = [];
        document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e) {
            if (e.code === 'Space') {
                e.preventDefault();
                times.push(performance.now() / 1000); 
                updateApp();
            }
        });

        function updateApp() {
            if (times.length < 2) return;
            periods = [];
            for(let i=1; i<times.length; i++) periods.push(times[i] - times[i-1]);
            document.getElementById('times').innerHTML = periods.map((p,i) => `<li>T${i+1}: ${p.toFixed(3)} s</li>`).join('');
            
            if (periods.length >= 10) calculateK();
        }

        function calculateK() {
            const m = parseFloat(document.getElementById('mass').value);
            const um = parseFloat(document.getElementById('uMass').value);
            const n = periods.length;
            
            const T_mean = periods.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0) / n;
            const s_T = Math.sqrt(periods.reduce((a,b)=>a+Math.pow(b-T_mean,2),0) / (n-1));
            const uT = s_T / Math.sqrt(n);
            
            const f = 1 / T_mean;
            const uf = uT / Math.pow(T_mean, 2);

            const k = 4 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) * m / Math.pow(T_mean, 2);
            
            const dk_dm = 4 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) / Math.pow(T_mean, 2);
            const dk_dT = -8 * Math.pow(Math.PI, 2) * m / Math.pow(T_mean, 3);
            
            const uk = Math.sqrt(Math.pow(dk_dm * um, 2) + Math.pow(dk_dT * uT, 2));
            
            const pm = (Math.pow(dk_dm * um, 2) / Math.pow(uk, 2) * 100).toFixed(1);
            const pT = (Math.pow(dk_dT * uT, 2) / Math.pow(uk, 2) * 100).toFixed(1);

            document.getElementById('results').innerHTML = `
                <h3>Results (n=${n}):</h3>
                <p>Frequency (f): ${f.toFixed(3)} ± ${uf.toFixed(3)} Hz</p>
                <p><strong>k = ${k.toFixed(2)} ± ${uk.toFixed(2)} N/m</strong></p>
                <p>Uncertainty Contribution: m (${pm}%), T (${pT}%)</p>
            `;
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>
